serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mutton Roganjosh is approximately 250 g, comprising 3–4 bone-in mutton pieces with gravy, as served in a restaurant or home meal context.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Roganjosh — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010475 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on meat-based curry with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Roganjosh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry dishes with similar macronutrient profiles (low carbohydrate 7.4 g/100g, high protein 14.1 g/100g, moderate fat 11.6 g/100g). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 7.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.6 g, classified as low GL. Estimation basis: protein and fat content substantially attenuate glycemic response; Kashmiri spices (fenugreek, ginger) may further reduce postprandial glucose. Reference: Atkinson FS et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g without adequate dietary fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 467 mg/100g falls in the borderline-high range (400–600 mg); potassium at 210 mg/100g provides partial offset but dietary fibre at 0.3 g/100g is insufficient to meet dual rescue criteria — individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 150 g or less.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.4 g/100g) and high protein (14.1 g/100g) attenuating glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Carbohydrate content is low at 7.4 g/100g and sugar at 4.7 g/100g is well below the 15 g threshold; the high protein content slows gastric emptying and supports postprandial blood glucose stability — pair with a high-fibre side dish such as dal or non-starchy vegetables to compensate for the very low fibre content of 0.3 g/100g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 85 mg/100g and saturated fat from red meat; sodium 467 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mutton is a red meat with cholesterol at 85 mg/100g and inherent saturated fat; combined with sodium at 467 mg/100g, regular or large-portion consumption is not recommended for individuals with established cardiovascular disease — limit to occasional small servings (≤150 g) and pair with vegetables to improve the overall dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6) from red meat and sodium 467 mg/100g exceeding RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mutton is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokines; sodium at 467 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold — individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish and consider substituting mutton with chicken or legumes while retaining the anti-inflammatory spice blend.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Roganjosh (also spelled Rogan Josh or Roghan Josh) is a classic Kashmiri slow-braised mutton curry originating from the Kashmir Valley of northern India. The dish features bone-in mutton pieces cooked in a rich, deeply coloured gravy made from Kashmiri red chili (which imparts vivid colour without extreme heat), whole aromatic spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf), fennel seeds, ginger, garlic, onion, and yogurt. The cooking medium is traditionally mustard oil or ghee. The meat is braised until tender, absorbing the layered spice profile. The flavour is complex — warm, mildly spicy, aromatic, and slightly tangy from the yogurt — with a silky, oil-enriched gravy. Texture is tender and yielding with gelatinous collagen from slow cooking. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.1 g protein, 11.6 g fat, 7.4 g carbohydrate, 4.7 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, 467 mg sodium, 210 mg potassium, 50 mg calcium, and 85 mg cholesterol. The dish is high in protein and moderate in fat, making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in controlled portions, but the elevated sodium content (467 mg/100g) makes it unsuitable for hypertension without salt reduction. The anti-inflammatory spice blend (ginger, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom) provides polyphenols, though these do not overcome the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid burden of red meat for rheumatoid arthritis patients. Roganjosh is a centrepiece dish in Kashmiri Wazwan feasts and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and globally.

What spices are in rogan josh?

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010475, review_status 'reviewed', confidence 0.95). The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence sub-component) but the overall record confidence remains high due to the exact nutrient source match.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Mutton Roganjosh preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010475) as of 2026-04-16. GI value of 30 is an analogous estimate based on comparable meat-based curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Roganjosh was found. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (30 × 7.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.6 g (low). Halal status assessed as likely halal given mutton preparation context in Indian cuisine; formal halal certification not confirmed from source data. Chinese name is a transliteration approximation; no established standard Chinese name for this dish was found in approved sources.
